method_atom: A framework for analyzing a diffusion / random-walk dynamical process on a discrete network whose regular short-range connectivity is augmented by a sparse set of long-range random links drawn with a tunable distance-dependent probability. The added random links are treated perturbatively around a mean-field limit, distinguishing a fast-disorder (annealed) regime where the links are re-drawn quickly from a slow-disorder (quenched) regime where they are effectively static, and their effect is captured with self-consistent perturbation theory and renormalization to obtain asymptotic scaling, with spectral diagonalization of the network Laplacian for numerical confirmation and a finite-size-scaling construction that maps the crossovers and the phase diagram of transient versus recurrent regimes.
object_atom: Small-world networks — a regular lattice interaction topology to which distance-dependent long-range random links are added — as models for transport on randomly folded polymer chains, synchronization in task-completion networks, and gradient-driven transport on networks.
brief_reason: The perturbative/renormalization treatment of sparse long-range disorder around a mean-field limit, including the annealed-vs-quenched distinction and Laplacian-spectrum finite-size scaling, is the transferable machinery, while the specific small-world network built on a lattice (and its polymer/synchronization/transport motivations) is the domain-specific object.
